title: podman logs colours the terminal persistently
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

The font colour of the terminal session continues to be the same as the last log entry. Only `reset` or `echo -ne '\033c'` or similar resets the colour.

If the command is part of a script this behaviour can get confusing...

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman logs <container>`

**Describe the results you received:**

The font colour of the terminal session continues to be the same as the last log entry. 

**Describe the results you expected:**

!(The font colour of the terminal session continues to be the same as the last log entry.)

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.0.4
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.14.6
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: `podman logs` itself does not use colors itself, but it will quite happily write any terminal control sequences (to include colors) that the container wrote to the logs. Can you try with another container from a different image and see if the problem persists?

I suppose we can probably save terminal state before running logs, and restore it when we exit, but I'm not sure if that actual affects current displayed color, that's more of a terminal emulator setting AFAIK.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@Luap99 commented in a PR

```
I understand why someone want this fixed by podman but I don't think it would make much sense.

    Someone might have set their color code to red for example and they wouldn't want it to be reset by podman.
    Other linux utilities like cat and tail don't do this either. Just try:

$ echo -e '\033[0;31mtest' > tmp
$ cat tmp

    I think it is very unlikely to end up in this state. If a program writes color codes to the terminal they would normally reset them.
    You can reset this yourself automatically for all programs if you put the reset code into your $PS1.
```
 I agree and think this is a not something Podman should fix.
